frameworks.name: task-specific neural coding
frameworks.one_line: single neurons participate in only some tasks, not all

frameworks.name: context-dependent neural representation
frameworks.one_line: physical context, not task identity, drives neuronal recruitment

core_question: Do individual parietal cortex neurons participate in multiple behavioral tasks, and what determines their participation?
key_assumptions: the same neurons can be tracked across multiple task sessions
physical apparatus context can be dissociated from task demands
manipulations: two visual decision tasks (steering wheel vs virtual T-maze)
recording in same apparatus without task stimuli
hybrid task combining steering-wheel context with T-maze visuals
key_findings: neurons active during T-maze task were typically inactive during steering-wheel task and vice versa
recording without task stimuli yielded the same neuronal specificity as during the task
hybrid task engaged the same neurons as the steering-wheel task, not the T-maze task
interpretations: parietal neuron participation is task specific
task specificity is determined by physical context rather than task demands
